metadata.summary: Produce win/loss analysis, pricing intelligence, and market-positioning signals by triangulating closed-deal records, buyer interviews, and competitor positioning.

content.title: Competitive Intelligence (Market-Researcher Lens)
content.summary: Competitive intelligence at the market-researcher level produces three deliverables the general CI loop does not cover: win/loss analysis (coding closed-deal records into structured loss reasons), pricing intelligence (triangulating list price against realized ACV from G2 disclosures and Vendr/Tropic benchmarks), and market-positioning signals (clustering competitor hero copy and buyer language by job-to-be-done). The core rule: every loss-reason row requires at least one verbatim buyer-side quote with speaker attribution — rep notes alone never justify a loss code.

why.p: Win/loss self-report bias causes sales reps to over-attribute losses to "price" when buyers actually leave for product fit, integration risk, or relationship gaps. Posted list price diverges from realized close price by 15-35% in B2B SaaS. Positioning language extracted from exec decks reflects aspiration, not buyer perception. Systematic triangulation across win/loss records, pricing page snapshots, and review-site text produces CI that drives decisions instead of confirming existing beliefs.

list.item: Deal review backlog has more than 20 closed-lost/closed-won opportunities in 60 days with unstructured CRM notes
Pricing committee meets quarterly and needs a defensible benchmark per segment
Inbound positioning brief is stalling because category language is contested
Repositioning, rebrand, or new-segment launch where messaging needs evidence from buyer language
Pre-Series-B/C: investors ask for a defensible win-rate trend with reasons-coded loss data
Fewer than 10 closed deals per quarter — sample too small; use customer interviews instead
Self-serve / PLG product with no sales conversations — switch to churn analytics and cancel-survey
Pricing locked by multi-year MSAs for the next 12 months — pricing intel has no decision to inform
Single-product, single-segment startup — positioning emerges from PMF interviews
Where buyer interviews are blocked by NDA — synthesize from public review sites, do not fabricate

section.name: win-loss-analysis
section.p: Rules for coding closed deals into structured loss reasons with buyer-side quote requirements and confidence scoring.

rule.statement: Every loss-reason row requires at least one verbatim buyer-side quote with speaker role, company size, and segment. Rep notes alone are insufficient — set confidence to 2 or below and flag for interview.
rule.rationale: Win/loss self-report bias: sales reps over-attribute losses to "price" when buyers actually leave for product fit, integration risk, or relationship gaps. Buyer-side quotes override rep attribution.

section.name: loss-reason-taxonomy
section.p: Maintain maximum 8 loss reasons in the taxonomy. Never expand silently — taxonomy additions require a documented decision. Deals that do not fit get "other" and go to human triage.

list.item: price: buyer explicitly cited price or total cost of ownership as primary reason
product_fit: feature gap, workflow mismatch, or integration depth insufficient
integration: specific technical integration with an existing system blocked the deal
relationship: incumbent vendor loyalty, existing executive relationship, or trust deficit
timing: not in active evaluation cycle; budget cycle mismatch; internal priority change
incumbent: existing solution "good enough"; switching cost too high
no_decision: deal stalled — budget frozen, stakeholder turnover, internal politics
other: reason does not fit taxonomy; requires manual triage

rule.statement: Maintain maximum 8 loss reasons in the taxonomy. Never expand silently — taxonomy additions require a documented decision. Deals that do not fit get "other" and go to human triage.
rule.rationale: Taxonomy drift breaks cross-period trend lines. A reason added in Q3 cannot be compared to Q1 data unless it is backfilled consistently.

rule.statement: Treat "no_decision" as its own outcome, not as a loss. Report stalled-pipeline rate separately from competitive loss rate.
rule.rationale: Mixing no-decision with competitive losses inflates the win-rate denominator and makes pipeline health look worse than it is. Leadership uses the wrong metric to judge sales effectiveness.

section.name: confidence-scoring
section.p: Score each loss record by evidence quality. Buyer-side interviews outweigh rep notes.

list.item: 5: direct buyer interview conducted by a non-deal-team member; verbatim quotes with attribution
4: buyer-submitted survey response or written post-mortem from buyer
3: rep notes include at least one direct buyer quote (not paraphrase)
2: rep notes only, no buyer direct quote — flag for interview
1: inferred from behavior (no response after pricing, chose competitor without explanation)

rule.statement: Run buyer-side win/loss interviews on at least 30% of lost deals above $50K ACV. Use Clozd or an internal interviewer who never sat on the deal — reps cannot interview their own losses.
rule.rationale: Rep-interviewed losses produce systematically biased data defending the rep's narrative. Independent interviewers surface the actual buyer motivations that drove the decision.

rule.statement: Stratify all win/loss analysis by segment and deal size. "We lose 60% of deals" is meaningless; "we lose 80% of mid-market deals against Vendor X when integration matters" is actionable.
rule.rationale: Blended win rates hide segment-specific patterns that require different responses. A 60% blended rate could mean 90% win in enterprise and 30% win in SMB — opposite strategic implications.

section.name: pricing-intelligence
section.p: Triangulating list price against realized ACV, packaging diff detection, and AB-test interference rules.

rule.statement: Triangulate competitor pricing three ways: posted list price, G2 reviewer-disclosed spend, and ex-customer or buyer interview. Publish the median and the spread — not a single number.
rule.rationale: B2B SaaS discounting averages 15-35% off list price. Published list price is aspirational; realized ACV from buyer disclosures is the actual competitive benchmark. Single-source pricing intel is misleading.

list.item: 1st — Ex-customer or buyer interview: actual invoiced amount, contract length, included seats/usage
2nd — Vendr/Tropic realized-price indexes: negotiated benchmarks from procurement platforms
3rd — G2 reviewer-disclosed spend: reviewer-reported price band with contract renewal status
4th — Pricing page scrape: list price, requires 3+ independent fetches over 14 days to confirm
5th — Press releases or analyst reports: directional only; cite as "secondary"

rule.statement: For marketplaces, always compute all-in take rate: commission + payment processing + seller subscription + ad platform fees. Never report headline commission alone.
rule.rationale: Headline commission (e.g., Etsy 6.5%) understates the real take by 2x or more when seller subscriptions, payment fees, and ad revenue are included. All-in rate is what sellers actually experience.

rule.statement: Require at least 3 independent fetches of a competitor pricing page over 14 days before declaring a pricing change. Single-fetch divergence is likely AB testing, not a price change.
rule.rationale: Competitors run pricing AB tests continuously; a daily fetch sees noise. Premature "competitor dropped prices" signals based on one fetch trigger incorrect strategic responses.

section.name: packaging-schema
section.p: Normalize all competitor pricing to a canonical schema: tier name, included seats or usage quota, overage rate, add-on price, contract length options, billing period (monthly/annual), currency, and source URL with retrieval date. Without normalization, cross-competitor comparisons mix incompatible denominators.

rule.statement: Pin currency, billing period, unit (per-seat / per-org / per-usage), and contract length in the pricing schema. "$10/month" without these fields cannot be compared across competitors.
rule.rationale: Currency and term ambiguity is the number-one cause of pricing benchmark errors. A per-seat monthly price cannot be added to a per-org annual price without normalization.

section.name: positioning-signals
section.p: Extracting and clustering positioning signals from buyer language, competitor copy, and analyst mentions by job-to-be-done.

rule.statement: Positioning extracted from buyer reviews outweighs positioning declared by competitor marketing copy. Hero copy reflects aspiration; G2 reviews reflect what buyers actually experience. The gap is your wedge.
rule.rationale: Competitors write hero copy for the positioning they want; buyers describe the product they got. The gap between declared positioning and buyer-described positioning is where an entrant can credibly differentiate.

list.item: 1st — Buyer win/loss interview quotes (highest buyer-centricity, smallest sample)
2nd — G2/TrustRadius review headlines and "what problems does this product solve" sections
3rd — Reddit and community forum discussions where buyers compare tools
4th — Analyst quadrant/wave positioning language (Gartner, Forrester) — directional, lagging
5th — Competitor homepage hero copy, "compare to" pages — aspirational, least reliable

section.name: jtbd-clustering
section.p: Extract verbatim quotes from buyers and competitors before running any LLM summarization. Cluster by job-to-be-done using seed labels established at the beginning of the quarter. Do not let the model auto-assign cluster names — it collapses distinct categories (e.g., "agent platform" + "automation platform" + "iPaaS" into one cluster) and loses the wedge.

rule.statement: Use k-means with manual seed labels per quarter; reject auto-clustering output. Re-run with the same seed labels each quarter to maintain trend comparability.
rule.rationale: Auto-clustering by LLMs merges adjacent categories that are actually distinct buyer segments with different purchase criteria. Manual seed labels preserve the signal that category definitions are shifting.

rule.statement: Re-baseline positioning analysis every quarter. Publish dated artifacts (competitive-analysis-YYYY-MM-DD.md). A stale market map is worse than no map — it guides decisions toward the wrong target.
rule.rationale: Pricing, packaging, and positioning shift continuously. Sales enablement teams trust dated artifacts because they know what version they are working from.

section.name: antipatterns
section.p: Structural failure modes in win/loss, pricing, and positioning CI, with agent-specific mitigation rules.

antipattern.description: Mixing "no_decision" outcomes with competitive losses in win-rate calculations
antipattern.reason: Inflates the denominator of competitive loss rate; stalled-pipeline rate requires different treatment and different corrective action than competitive displacement.

antipattern.description: Using only rep notes without any buyer-side quote for loss coding
antipattern.reason: Rep notes systematically over-attribute losses to "price" and "competition" to avoid admitting process or product failures that reflect on rep performance.

antipattern.description: Reporting a single median price instead of a range (P25/P50/P75)
antipattern.reason: Single medians create false precision. The spread between P25 and P75 is often the strategic signal — it shows whether the market has standardized pricing or wide negotiation room.

antipattern.description: Anchoring on the loudest competitor in review-site analysis
antipattern.reason: Market-share leaders dominate review-site volume; emerging threats are invisible until they take a deal. Stratify by segment and deal-size, not raw mention count.

antipattern.description: Citing the Crayon "16% to 45% win rate" case study without attribution control
antipattern.reason: That uplift conflates CI rollout with new pricing, new battlecards, and new sales hires. Attributing it solely to CI misleads leadership about expected ROI.

antipattern.description: Agent invents a loss reason from rep notes without a buyer-side quote
antipattern.reason: Force the output schema to require at least 1 verbatim quote with a speaker field; reject rows where evidence_quotes is empty.

antipattern.description: Agent reports "no Enterprise tier exists" when competitor hides it behind "Contact Sales"
antipattern.reason: Mitigate with G2-disclosed price ranges and LinkedIn job posts mentioning ACV bands. Absence of a public pricing page does not mean absence of an Enterprise tier.

antipattern.description: Agent defends sales rep narrative ("buyer was confused about the pricing")
antipattern.reason: Use adversarial prompt framing: "Argue the buyer was right to choose the competitor; cite evidence." This surfaces the buyer-side perspective the rep omits.

antipattern.description: Agent paraphrases buyer quotes, losing attribution
antipattern.reason: Preserve speaker_role, company_size, and segment end-to-end, never the buyer's name. Paraphrased quotes are useless to leadership — they cannot be cited or challenged.

antipattern.description: Agent adds new loss-reason categories each run, breaking trend lines
antipattern.reason: Pin the loss-reason enum in the output schema; flag deals that do not fit for human triage rather than silently expanding the taxonomy.

template.purpose: Python script joining win/loss, pricing, and positioning event streams into competitive-analysis.md
